critique_points.point_001: The paper lacks explicit comparison and citations to recent state-of-the-art retrieval-augmented models leveraging heterogeneous knowledge bases, making it unclear how KiC advances beyond prior methods.
critique_points.point_002: There are no ablations comparing the top-1 hard selection knowledge selector to top-k or soft weighting alternatives, leaving unclear whether the hard routing limits performance or knowledge combination benefits.
critique_points.point_003: Details on knowledge memory construction and key-value encoding are insufficient, lacking concrete examples or standardized templates across all six knowledge categories, which hampers reproducibility.
critique_points.point_004: Zero-shot evaluation compares KiCLarge to much larger models without including parametric baselines of similar size and data exposure, risking confounding architectural gains with differences in pretraining or data.
critique_points.point_005: Fine-tuning baselines omit strong comparable models using the same backbone without knowledge augmentation, making it difficult to attribute gains specifically to knowledge integration.
critique_points.point_006: Key training and retrieval hyperparameters are not reported, hindering reproducibility of the differentiable knowledge selector training and validation of efficiency claims.
critique_points.point_007: The paper does not specify dataset versions, preprocessing, filtering criteria, or chunking strategies for the diverse external knowledge sources, complicating replication and impact assessment of dataset quality.
critique_points.point_008: The mixture-of-experts formulation lacks formal mathematical details and pseudocode describing how selector probabilities modulate output logits, how load balancing loss is integrated, and gradient flow, reducing clarity.

summary: This paper proposes Knowledge-in-Context (KiC), a semi-parametric language model that augments a text-to-text backbone with an external knowledge memory comprising six structured knowledge categories. An instance-adaptive knowledge selector chooses the most relevant category per input, enabling efficient retrieval and integration of diverse knowledge types. Reformulated as a mixture-of-experts model with differentiable training and load balancing, KiC achieves strong zero-shot and fine-tuned task performance, outperforming much larger baseline models and showing emergent abilities at smaller scales. The work demonstrates the value of structured knowledge and adaptive selection for building parameter-efficient, knowledgeable language models.
strengths: Introduces a novel semi-parametric LM architecture combining multiple structured knowledge sources with a parametric backbone.
Employs an instance-adaptive knowledge selector enabling dynamic retrieval of the most relevant knowledge category per input instance.
weaknesses: Lacks explicit comparisons to recent state-of-the-art retrieval-augmented models and comprehensive ablation studies of the knowledge selector design.
Insufficient reporting of key experimental details, dataset processing, and training hyperparameters reduces reproducibility and clarity.
questions: How does KiC perform when using top-k or soft knowledge selector strategies instead of hard top-1 selection?
Can the authors provide detailed examples of the key-value encoding schemes used for each knowledge category to aid reproducibility?
How would KiC scale in terms of retrieval latency and memory footprint when expanding knowledge categories or model size?
What mechanisms exist or are envisioned for updating or dynamically expanding the external knowledge memory post-training?
title: Knowledge-in-Context: Towards Knowledgeable Semi-Parametric Language Models
